item.title: Unit 2 - Introduction to Data, Measurements, and Data preprocessing
item.description: #data_miningThe Quality BottleneckGarbage In, Garbage OutYou cannot cook a Michelin-star meal with rotten ingredients. Up to 80% of data science/mining is just cleaning. Why Preprocessing? Accuracy: Handling Noise Completeness: Handling Missing Values Consistency: Handling Discrepancies Data ObjectsDefinition: Represents a specific entity. Contextual Examples Sales DB: Customers, store items, sales. Medical DB: Patients. University DB: Students, professors, courses. Synonyms for Data ObjectsYou may see these terms used interchangeably: Examples Instances Data Points Objects Data AttributesDefinition: A data field representing a characteristic or feature of a data object. One Concept, Many Names Dimension: Data Warehousing Attribute: Data Mining & Databases Feature: Machine Learning Variable: Statistics The Taxonomy of AttributesThere are four specific types of attributes you must know. Nominal Attributes "Names" Values represent categories, codes, or states. Also called: Categorical. Math: Mode only. Example: Blood Type (A, B, AB, O), Color (Red, Blue). Binary Attributes "0 or 1" Nominal attributes with only two states. Boolean: If states are True/False. Types of Binary: Symmetric: Both states carry equal weight (e.g., Gender). Asymmetric: Outcomes are not equally important. The rarer/important outcome is usually coded as 1 (e.g., HIV test result). Ordinal Attributes "Ranked" Values have a meaningful order. Constraint: The magnitude between values is unknown. Example: Drink Sizes (Small, Medium, Large), Disease Stage (I, II, III). Numeric Attributes "Quantitative" Measurable quantities (Integer or Real values). Scales: Interval-scaled: Equal-size units, NO true zero-point (e.g., Temperature in Celsius). Ratio-scaled: Inherent zero-point, allows multiples/ratios (e.g., Salary, Height). Continuity: Discrete: Finite or countably infinite set (Integers). Continuous: Real numbers (Floating-point).
item.guid: Data Mining/Midterms Reviewer/Reviewer Content/Unit 2/Unit 2 Content/Unit 2 - Introduction to Data, Measurements, and Data preprocessing.md

item.title: Unit 2 - Statistics of Data
item.description: #data_miningMeasures of Central TendencyGoal: Measure the location of the middle or center of a data distribution. "Where do most values fall?" The Three Measures of Central TendencyMean (The Balance Point)Formula: The weighted center. Pros: Most common measure. Cons: Highly sensitive to outliers (e.g., a billionaire enters a room). Median (The Border Divider)Definition: The middle value when data is sorted ( percentile). Position: Pros: Robust statistic (ignored outliers). Use Case: Skewed data (Income, House Prices). Approximated Medianused in massive datasets where sorting the entire set is computationally expensive Mode (The Popular Vote)Definition: The value that appears most frequently. Types: Unimodal, Bimodal, Multimodal. Pros: The only measure for Categorical/Nominal data. Use Case: Imputing missing categorical values. Data Distribution (Skewness)Skewness measures the asymmetry of the probability distribution. Pearson's Coefficient of Skewness: Logic: If Skewness > 1 or < -1, transformations (Log/Square Root) are needed for ML algorithms. Measures of DispersionGoal: Quantify the "Chaos". How are points scattered around the center? Basic MeasuresRange (The Extremes)Formula: The simplest measure of spread. Pros: Easy to calculate. Cons: Extremely sensitive to outliers as it only uses the two most extreme values. Percentiles ()Definition: Divides sorted data into 100 equal-sized groups. The -th percentile is the value below which of the data falls. Key Values: : The Median. : The threshold for the "Top 1%". Quartiles ()Definition: Three specific points that divide the ordered data set into four equal parts (quarters). ( Quartile / Percentile): Splits off the lowest 25% of data. ( Quartile / Percentile): The Median. ( Quartile / Percentile): Splits off the highest 25% (lowest 75%). The Boxplot (Five-Number Summary)Used to visualize distribution and outliers. The 5 Points: Minimum Median () Maximum Interquartile Range (IQR): Formula: Represents the middle 50% of data. The Outlier Test (Fences): Lower Fence: Upper Fence: Any data outside these fences is an outlier. Variance & Standard DeviationVariance (): Average squared distance from the mean. (Note the "Bessel's Correction" for sample data) Standard Deviation (): Interpretation: Low SD = clustered data; High SD = wide spread.
item.guid: Data Mining/Midterms Reviewer/Reviewer Content/Unit 2/Unit 2 Content/Unit 2 - Statistics of Data.md

item.title: Assignment 1
item.description: #sipp Scenario 1 Rajesh invests small amounts on the stock market. Last year he bought and successfully employed a software package to help him with his investments. Recently, he met Fundiswa who was also interested in using the software. Fundiswa borrowed the package, copied it and then returned it. Both vaguely knew that the software was proprietary but did not read up the details. Did Rajesh and Fundiswa do anything wrong, if so what? More generally, try to answer the following related questions. Should the software package have been lent? When is it justifiable to break the law? Bad law, inappropriate law or if the law is easy to break? Answer Generally speaking, no, the software shouldn't have been copied even if both of them agreed with the act, thus enabling fundiswa to use the software without paying for it, while both of them know that the software is proprietary. One could argue that since rajesh bought it, then he can do whatever he wants to 'his' software package, but this cant be right because of the end user license agreement and this isnt like physical goods where if you buy it, you physically can do whatever you want with it. But then, one could further argue that if buying isnt owning, then piracy isnt stealing, but then, as copyright infringement laws state--generally speaking--piracy is still illegal and unethical since it takes revenue from creators regardless of the licensing model. If youre a professional and can afford paying for the software youre using to earn, why risk yourself of getting caught in using a pirated software? However, i do believe it is justifiable to break the law as long as you're using it for educational use, and not for professional use--more especially if the software is from an industry giant who monopolizes the market. students or learners shouldn't be criminalized for accessing the tools they need to learn the trade, provided they aren't profiting from them yet. Learning to use softwares that professionals use shouldnt be locked behind a paywall. Generally speaking, no, the software shouldn't have been copied, even if both parties agreed to the act. Since both knew the software was proprietary, enabling Fundiswa to use it without paying is problematic. One could argue that since Rajesh bought it, he should be able to do what he wants with "his" package. But this ignores the End User License Agreement (EULA). Unlike physical goods, where buying implies total ownership, software is licensed, not sold. While some argue that "if buying isn't owning, then piracy isn't stealing," copyright laws still classify this as infringement. It is unethical because it deprives creators of revenue, regardless of the licensing model. If you are a professional who can afford the software and you are using it to earn money, why risk getting caught using pirated tools? It is unprofessional to generate profit from a tool you didn't pay for. However, I do believe it is justifiable to break the law when it comes to educational use—specifically not for professional gain. Especially if the software comes from an industry giant that monopolizes the market. Learning to use the tools that professionals use shouldn't be locked behind a paywall. Students or learners shouldn't be criminalized for accessing the tools they need to learn the trade, provided they aren't profiting from them yet.Scenario 2 Consider the case where Tisetso sells hardware and software to over 100 000 customers per year. She has 10 years of experience. As part of the billing process she keeps information on customers. She buys and uses a data mining tool to derive useful information about her client’s information such as postal codes, credit card numbers, ID numbers, etc. Most of this information identifies groups and not individuals. She can then use the information to market her wares more efficiently. Is this ethical since customers did not give her the information for this purpose? Should the customer be notified? Is there a need for establishment of a policy? If so, what should this policy looks like? Professional responsibility (professional Ethics): Do professionals have a responsibility to ensure computing serves humanity well? Answer Should the customer be notified? Generally speaking, yes, the customers should be notified. Even though Tisetso is analyzing groups rather than specific individuals, she is repurposing private data—like ID and credit card numbers—that was originally collected only for billing. One could argue that since the data is anonymized and helps her business run efficiently, it is harmless. However, this can’t be right because the customers never consented to their data being used for marketing. It is a breach of trust to use personal information for a secondary purpose without transparency. Is there a need for the establishment of a policy? Absolutely. Without a clear policy, she is operating in a gray area that puts both her reputation and her customers' privacy at risk. The policy should be simple and explicit, likely included in the Terms of Service. It should state something like: "We collect billing data, but we may also use anonymized aggregate data to improve our services." Crucially, there should be an option for customers to "opt-out" if they are uncomfortable with this. Professional Responsibility: With 10 years of experience, Tisetso is a seasoned professional who should know better than to exploit a loophole. Professionals have a responsibility to ensure computing serves humanity well, and that includes respecting user privacy. If you are using powerful data mining tools solely to maximize profit from unsuspecting clients, you are prioritizing revenue over ethics. Technology should be used to build trust, not to exploit the data people gave you in confidence. Scenario 3 A student, Gert, posts sex fantasies on a blog (alike blogger.com), called Ling’s Journey. The stories are fictional, but Gert named the main character, Ling, after a real student. In the story, he described the rape, torture and murder of Ling. He is also member of a social media newsgroup (alike Facebook), discussing sex acts. An alumnus saw this and reported it to the University as well as to the social media companies. Gert was then arrested and held in custody and the social media companies suspended his account. He was charged with transmitting communication of threat to injure another person, and violating community policies of the respective social media platforms. The charges were eventually dropped and his accounts reinstated. Did Gert really do anything wrong? Should self-censorship be enforced. Who decides what is acceptable? Is the social media software company responsible for the content on their site? Should this data—the blog posts and the social media newsgroup chats—be kept online accessible indefinitely? Is there a need for a public policy on freedom of expression on social media, as compared to, say, printed hardcopy books and magazines? Answer Did Gert do anything wrong? / Should self-censorship be enforced? Generally speaking, yes, Gert did something wrong. While he has the right to write fiction, naming the victim after a real fellow student crosses the line from creative expression to targeted harassment. One could argue that "it’s just a story" and he has freedom of speech, but this can’t be right because the content depicts specific violence against a real person he knows. That looks less like fiction and more like a threat. I do believe self-censorship should be enforced when your expression directly endangers or psychologically harms a specific individual. Who decides? Initially, the individual needs to exercise judgment, but ultimately, the platform holders and the law must intervene when safety is compromised. Is the social media company responsible? Technically, social media platforms are usually seen as hosts, not publishers, so they aren't legally responsible for every post. However, they are responsible for enforcing their own Community Guidelines. If they allow threatening content to remain up after it has been reported, they are failing in their duty to maintain a safe environment. They shouldn't be held liable for the upload itself, but they should be liable for inaction once notified. Should this data be kept online indefinitely? No, absolutely not in this specific case. Since the content names a real person and depicts them being tortured and murdered, it violates the subject's rights and safety. It should be removed. However, for general content that doesn't target individuals or break laws, one could argue it should remain accessible as an archive of free speech. But when it comes to targeted harassment, the "right to be forgotten" or safety concerns must override the desire to archive data. Is there a need for public policy on social media vs. print? Yes, there is a need for a specific policy for social media because the impact is different from print. Printed books have editors and a slower distribution process, whereas social media is instant, global, and lacks pre-publication filters. A threat in a book is bad, but a threat on social media can go viral and incite real-world harm in minutes. Therefore, policies need to be stricter and faster-acting for digital platforms to account for the speed and reach of the medium.
item.guid: Social Issues and Professional Practice in IT & Computing/Assignment 1.md

item.title: Architectural Models
item.description: #parallel_computing The most widely used models are:Client request services, Servers provide services request-response based communication Centralized servers clients are dependent on server availability easy to manage and secure potential bottleneck at the server (Web apps, email sys., online banking sys.) All nodes both act as clients and servers No permanent central sever Nodes communicate directly with each other (among the peers) decentralized architecture highly scalable fault tolerance through redundancy harder to manage and secure (file sharing sys., blockchain, torrents, drop box) Allows code to move across different machines instead of only data execution happens where resources or data reside. Reduced network traffic dynamic execution environments security risks if not properly controlled (java applets, web scripts, serverless functions)
item.guid: Parallel Computing/02-03-2026/Architectural Models.md

item.title: Fundamental Models
item.description: #parallel_computing describe the assumptions and guarantees of a distributed system They focus on:defines how processes communicate and coordinate with each other considers timing, message ordering, and synchronizationAssumptions: no global clock message delays are unpredictable processes run concurrently Different components:Deals with passing messages that may contain data, instructions, a service request, or process synchronization between different computing nodes. Can be synchronous or asynchronous depending on types of tasks and processes.pub/sub systemcommunication paradigm that has an ability to invoke new process or a method on a remote process as if it were a local procedure call.how systems can fail and what kinds of failures are expectedFour types of failures: Crash failures - process or node that unexpectedly stops functioning Omission failures - involves a loss of message, resulting in absence of required communication Timing failures - the process deviates from its expected time quantum and may lead to delays Byzantine failures - the process may send malicious or unexpected messages that conflict with the set protocols Failure handling strategies replication timeouts and retries faut detection mechanisms how the system protects: Data Resources Communication Security threats eavesdropping spoofing Denial of service Data tampering Security mechanisms password-based authentication - users provide unique password to prove ones identity public-key cryptography - entities possess a private key and a corresponding public key to verify their authenticity multi-factor authentication - multiple factors such as passwords, biometrics etc. process pf transforming data into a format that is unreadable without... secure communication protocols
item.guid: Parallel Computing/02-03-2026/Fundamental Models.md

item.title: Physical Models
item.description: #parallel_computingPoints where the data is coming from/ being sent to.Communication Channels between nodes and intermediate devices Point-to-point links - Connection between only 2 nodes Broadcast links - allows 1 node to transmit data to multiple nodes simultaneously Multi-Access links - Arrangement of nodes and links in a distributed system.Set rules and procedures for transmitting data from the links. Allows nodes to communicate and interpret data.
item.guid: Parallel Computing/02-03-2026/Physical Models.md

item.title: Activity
item.description:
#parallel_computing
Fundamental Modelsscenario 1 centralized server overload the main verification server becomes unreachable what type of failure is this? Crash failure Is the entire system unusable? yes, because only has a single point of failure What design assumption failed? The assumption that the server is always available and has sufficient capacity to handle all concurrent client requests. scenario 2 network partition some lgus can access the system, others cant what type of failure is this? Omission failure why is this difficult to detect? It is difficult to detect because there's a loss of message so you dont know what went wrong. Theres an absence of required communication scenario 3 delayed responses Is this a failure or an interaction issue? It is a failure, specifically timing failure where the process deviates from its expected time quantum what risk does delay introduce? to the system, time delays can contribute to the server having a high traffic causing DoS. Even a small delays can add up and significantly delay citizen's processes.

item.title: [Unit 4] - Classification Basics & ML Components
item.description: #nlpGoal: Memorize the core tasks of text classification and the fundamental architectural components of a supervised machine learning model.Text Categorization (Classification)The task of assigning a label or category to a text or document from a predefined set of classes. We use Supervised Machine Learning, meaning we train the system using a dataset where the correct answers (Gold Labels) are already provided. Memory Hook 🧠Remember the 4 most common text classification tasks with S.S.L.A.: Sentiment Analysis (Is this review positive, negative, or neutral?) Spam Detection (Is this email spam or not-spam?) Language ID (Is this text written in English, Tagalog, or Spanish?) Authorship Attribution (Did Shakespeare or Marlowe write this text?) The 4 Components of an ML ClassifierEvery supervised machine learning classifier consists of these four distinct parts. Memory Hook 🧠Think of the acronym F.C.O.O. (Fast Computers Optimize Outputs): Feature Representation: How the input text is represented as numbers (e.g., counting how many times the word "awesome" appears). Classification Function: The math equation that computes the estimated class (e.g., Sigmoid or Softmax). Objective Function (Loss): The formula that measures how far off the system's guess is from the true gold label (e.g., Cross-Entropy Loss). Optimization Algorithm: The method used to iteratively adjust the weights to minimize the loss (e.g., Stochastic Gradient Descent). Features, Weights, and Bias Input Observation (): The text being classified, converted into a vector of numbers. Features (): Specific properties we extract from the input (e.g., = count of positive words, = presence of an exclamation mark). Weights (): Learned real numbers that tell us how important a specific feature is to the final decision. (Positive weight = pushes toward class 1; Negative weight = pushes away). Bias (): Also called the intercept. A baseline number added to the weighted features to shift the decision boundary. Use Case: In sentiment analysis, the word "abysmal" would be an input feature () that the model eventually assigns a highly negative weight ().
item.guid: Natural Language Processing/Unit 4/Unit 4 Contents/[Unit 4] - Classification Basics & ML Components.md

item.title: [Unit 4] - Evaluation Metrics (Precision, Recall, F1)
item.description: #nlpGoal: Memorize the Confusion Matrix vocabulary and why we must use Precision, Recall, and F1 instead of plain Accuracy.⚠️ The "Nothing is Pie" Problem (Why Accuracy Fails)If you have 1,000,000 tweets, and only 100 are about pie (the positive class), a stupid classifier that just labels every single tweet as "Negative" will have an Accuracy of 99.99%. However, it found exactly zero pie tweets. Accuracy is useless for imbalanced datasets.The Confusion Matrix VocabularyWhen comparing system outputs to human Gold Labels: True Positive (TP): System said Yes, Gold said Yes. (Correct hit) True Negative (TN): System said No, Gold said No. (Correct rejection) False Positive (FP): System said Yes, Gold said No. (False Alarm / Type I error) False Negative (FN): System said No, Gold said Yes. (Missed it / Type II error) Formula Explainer: Precision, Recall, & F11. Precision (Quality) English: Out of all the things the system claimed were positive, how many were actually right? (A high precision means few false alarms). 2. Recall (Coverage) English: Out of all the actual positive items that existed in the real world, how many did our system manage to find? (A high recall means few missed items). 3. F1-Measure (Balance) English: The harmonic mean of Precision and Recall. It gives you a single metric that balances both. (Uses harmonic mean so a terrible score in either P or R drags the whole F1 score down drastically). How to Solve (Example Scenario): Scenario: You built a Spam Filter. You test it, and find the following: TP = 8 (Spam emails correctly caught) FP = 2 (Normal, safe emails wrongly sent to spam) FN = 4 (Actual spam emails that snuck into the normal inbox) Precision = 0.80 (Meaning: 80% of the emails sitting in the spam folder are actually spam). Recall = 0.67 (Meaning: Your filter only caught 67% of all the actual spam that existed). F1-Measure = 0.73 (A balanced final score reflecting both coverage and quality). Multi-class Evaluation: Macro vs. MicroWhen you have more than 2 classes (e.g., Urgent, Normal, Spam), how do you average the Precision and Recall?
item.guid: Natural Language Processing/Unit 4/Unit 4 Contents/[Unit 4] - Evaluation Metrics (Precision, Recall, F1).md

item.title: [Unit 4] - Learning, Loss & Gradient Descent
item.description: #nlpGoal: Memorize how the model evaluates its own mistakes (Loss) and the algorithm it uses to physically update its weights to get smarter (Gradient Descent).Formula Explainer: Cross-Entropy LossThe standard Objective Function (Loss Function) for Logistic Regression. English Translation: This formula measures the "distance" between the model's predicted probability () and the true gold label (, which is either exactly 1 or 0). If the true label , the right half of the formula drops out. We just want to be as close to 1 as possible to minimize the loss. Bottom line conceptual rule: High probability of the correct answer = Low Loss. Low probability of the correct answer = High Loss (Punishment). Gradient Descent ConceptHow do we find the minimum possible loss? Imagine you are hiking blindfolded in a canyon and want to find the lowest point (minimum loss). Feel the slope of the ground under your feet (compute the gradient). Step in the opposite direction of the upward slope. Repeat until the ground is flat (you've hit the minimum). ⚠️ ConvexityLuckily, the loss function for Logistic Regression is convex (shaped like a perfect bowl). This guarantees there is only one global minimum, so gradient descent will never get permanently stuck in a fake minimum.The Learning Rate () & HyperparametersWhen taking a step down the gradient, how big of a step do you take? This is controlled by the Learning Rate (). If is too high: You take massive steps and overshoot the minimum entirely. If is too low: You take microscopic steps and it takes forever to finish training. Hyperparameter: The learning rate is a hyperparameter. Unlike weights (which the algorithm learns on its own), hyperparameters are dials and knobs set by the human programmer before training starts.Comparison: Batch Sizes in Gradient DescentHow many examples does the algorithm look at before deciding to update its weights?
item.guid: Natural Language Processing/Unit 4/Unit 4 Contents/[Unit 4] - Learning, Loss & Gradient Descent.md

item.title: [Unit 4] - Logistic Regression, Sigmoid & Softmax
item.description: #nlpGoal: Memorize the functions used to turn raw weighted sums (logits) into clean, usable probabilities for both 2-class and multi-class problems.⚠️ The Problem with Raw Sums (Logits)In Logistic Regression, we multiply features by weights and add the bias: . The raw result () is called the logit. The Problem: can be any number from to . It is NOT a probability. We must squash it into a range between and to make a decision!Formula Explainer: The Sigmoid Function (Binary)Used for Binary Logistic Regression (exactly 2 classes, e.g., Spam vs Not-Spam). English Translation: Take your raw score () and pass it through the Sigmoid () function. It maps any real value into the range (0, 1). If is highly positive, the output approaches . If is highly negative, the output approaches . Because it's an "S" curve, it squashes extreme outlier values flat. Decision Boundary: We usually say if , classify as Class 1. Otherwise, Class 0.Formula Explainer: The Softmax Function (Multinomial)Used for Multinomial Logistic Regression (3 or more classes, e.g., Positive, Negative, Neutral). English Translation: Instead of a single weight vector, you have a weight matrix containing a prototype vector for each class. Softmax takes the raw scores for all classes, exponentiates them (to make them positive and exaggerate the biggest one), and divides by the sum of all exponentiated scores. Result: It outputs a vector of probabilities that all sum up to exactly . 💡 One-Hot VectorIn multinomial classification, the true gold label is represented as a one-hot vector. If Class 2 is the correct answer out of 4 classes, the vector is [0, 1, 0, 0].Comparison: Sigmoid vs. Softmax
item.guid: Natural Language Processing/Unit 4/Unit 4 Contents/[Unit 4] - Logistic Regression, Sigmoid & Softmax.md

item.title: [Unit 4] - Testing, Harms & Regularization
item.description: #nlpGoal: Memorize the methods for ensuring a model actually generalizes to the real world without overfitting, and understand the ethical impacts (harms) of text classification.Data Splits & Cross-Validation Training Set: Used to update the weights. Devset (Development): Used to test tweaks and tune hyperparameters during development. Test Set: Held completely blind until the very end to prove the model generalizes. 10-Fold Cross-Validation: When you don't have enough data to spare a giant test set, you split your data into 10 chunks (folds). You train on 9 folds, test on the 1st. Then train on a different 9, test on the 2nd. Do this 10 times and average the test scores.Statistical Significance (Paired Bootstrap Test)If Model A gets an F1 of .70 and Model B gets an F1 of .50, is A really better, or was it a lucky accident based on this specific test set? Null Hypothesis (): Assumes A is not actually better than B (the difference is ). p-value: The probability that we would see our score difference just by random luck if the Null Hypothesis were true. If the p-value is tiny (e.g., ), we reject the null hypothesis and say the result is statistically significant. Bootstrap Test: A non-parametric test used in NLP. It repeatedly draws thousands of random virtual test samples (with replacement) from the original test set to calculate the p-value. 💡 Overfitting & RegularizationOverfitting: When a model memorizes the training data too perfectly, including all the random noise, meaning it fails terribly on unseen test data. Solution: Regularization (Adding a penalty to the loss function for having weights that are too large). Avoiding Harms in ClassificationMachine learning systems inherit and amplify the biases present in human training data. Representational Harms: Demeaning a social group or perpetuating stereotypes (e.g., a sentiment classifier assigning negative sentiment simply because a sentence contains an African American name). Censorship / Silencing: A toxicity detector incorrectly flagging non-toxic sentences that mention minority identities (e.g., "blind", "gay") as hate speech, thereby nudging people to stop discussing those topics online. Use Case (Model Cards): To combat this, researchers release Model Cards: formal documents explaining exactly what data a model was trained on, its intended use, and its performance discrepancies across different demographic groups.
item.guid: Natural Language Processing/Unit 4/Unit 4 Contents/[Unit 4] - Testing, Harms & Regularization.md

item.title: NLP Unit 4 Overview
item.description:
#nlpThis unit introduces the foundational concept of assigning categories to text. You will learn the mechanics of Logistic Regression (both binary and multinomial), how models learn through gradient descent, and the metrics we use to evaluate if a classifier is actually doing its job properly (without being fooled by unbalanced data).Click the links below to view the specific reviewers:
[Unit 4] - Classification Basics & ML Components Topics: Supervised Learning, Core NLP Tasks, 4 Components of ML Classifiers
[Unit 4] - Logistic Regression, Sigmoid & Softmax Topics: Features, Weights, Bias, Sigmoid (Binary), Softmax (Multinomial)
[Unit 4] - Learning, Loss & Gradient Descent Topics: Cross-Entropy Loss, Gradient Descent, Learning Rate, Batch Types
[Unit 4] - Evaluation Metrics (Precision, Recall, F1) Topics: Confusion Matrix, Accuracy flaw, Precision, Recall, F1, Macro vs. Microaveraging
[Unit 4] - Testing, Harms & Regularization Topics: Cross-validation, Bootstrap Test, Overfitting, L1 vs L2 Regularization, Representational Harms Key TakeawayClassification is the heartbeat of NLP. Logistic regression solves classification by multiplying input features by learned weights, summing them, and squashing the result into a probability (using Sigmoid or Softmax). We evaluate these models not just by how often they are right (Accuracy), but by how well they find what we actually care about (Precision/Recall), while carefully ensuring they don't overfit the training data or perpetuate societal harms.
item.guid: Natural Language Processing/Unit 4/NLP Unit 4 Overview.md

item.title: [Unit 3] - Evaluation & Perplexity
item.description: #nlpGoal: Understand how to test a language model and calculate its Perplexity, the standard metric for language model performance.Evaluation Methods & Data Splits Extrinsic (in-vivo): Testing the LM by embedding it in a real application (e.g., measuring machine translation accuracy). Expensive but definitive. Intrinsic (in-vitro): Testing the LM on a standalone metric (Perplexity) independent of a downstream task. The Golden Rule of Data Splits: Training Set: Used to get the counts and probabilities. Dev Set (Development): Used to test and tune the model iteratively. Test Set: Used strictly once at the very end to gauge unseen generalization. ⚠️ Data Contamination (Training on the Test Set)If your test sentences leak into your training set, your model will assign them an artificially high probability. It invalidates your evaluation completely.Formula Explainer: Perplexity (PP)Formula: English Translation: Perplexity is the inverse probability of the test set, normalized by the number of words (). Because it uses the inverse probability (), a LOWER perplexity is BETTER. It means the model was less surprised by the text. How to Solve (Branching Factor Example): Think of perplexity as a "Weighted Average Branching Factor" (how many possible next words the model is guessing between). Scenario: A fake language has 3 words: Red, Blue, Green. A model assigns them equal probability ( each). Test Set : "red red red" () Find the probability of the sequence: Plug into perplexity formula: The exponents cancel out (). Result: . (The perplexity is 3, matching the branching factor). How to Solve (Bigram Chain Rule version): If you are given a list of bigram probabilities for a 3-word sentence (e.g., 0.5, 0.2, 0.1), you multiply them together (), take the inverse (), and then take the th root ().Memory Hook 🧠To remember the core relationship of Perplexity: L.I.P. Lower Is Preferred (Because it's the inverse of probability! High Probability = Low Perplexity). TipUse Case: When comparing an N-gram model to a modern LLM, engineers calculate the Perplexity of both on Wikipedia text. The one with the lower number is pushed to production.
item.guid: Natural Language Processing/Unit 3/Unit 3 Content/[Unit 3] - Evaluation & Perplexity.md

item.title: [Unit 3] - N-Grams & Probability Estimation
item.description: #nlpGoal: Master the ability to calculate the probability of a word (or entire sentence) using raw frequency counts and the Markov assumption.What is a Language Model?A Language Model (LM) is a machine learning model that predicts upcoming words by assigning a probability to a sequence of words: . To calculate the joint probability of a whole sentence, we rely on the Chain Rule of Probability.Formula Explainer: The Chain Rule applied to WordsFormula: English Translation: The probability of an entire sentence ( through ) is equal to the probability of the first word, times the probability of the second word given the first, times the probability of the third word given the first two, and so on. How to Solve: Sentence: "The water is blue" ⚠️ The Chain Rule ProblemWe cannot realistically compute because a sentence this long might never appear in our training data. This is where the Markov Assumption steps in.Formula Explainer: The Markov Assumption (Bigram)Formula: English Translation: Instead of looking at the entire history of the sentence, we approximate it by only looking at the last word (Bigram) or the last two words (Trigram). How to Solve (Sentence Approximation): Sentence: "The water is blue" (Note: <s\> is the start-of-sentence token).Formula Explainer: Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) for BigramsFormula: English Translation: To find the probability of word B coming after word A, you count how many times the exact phrase "A B" appears in the text, and divide it by the total number of times word A appears overall. (MLE maximizes the likelihood of the training set given the model). How to Solve: Corpus: 1. <s> I am Sam </s> 2. <s> Sam I am </s> 3. <s> I do not like green eggs </s> Question: What is ? Find : The phrase "I am" occurs 2 times (in sentence 1 and 2). Find : The word "I" occurs 3 times. Calculate: Question: What is ? Find : Sentences starting with "I" occur 2 times. Find : There are 3 sentences total. Calculate: Use Case: This MLE formula is the literal engine under the hood of basic predictive text features (like older smartphone keyboards predicting your next word).💡 Computational Trap: UnderflowWhen you multiply many small probabilities together (e.g., ), computers suffer from numerical underflow (the number gets too small for the CPU to track accurately). Solution: Always convert to Log Probabilities. Adding logs is mathematically equivalent to multiplying the original probabilities:
item.guid: Natural Language Processing/Unit 3/Unit 3 Content/[Unit 3] - N-Grams & Probability Estimation.md

item.title: [Unit 3] - Smoothing, Interpolation & Backoff
item.description: #nlpGoal: Solve the "Sparsity/Zeros" problem by reallocating probability mass using Laplace smoothing, Interpolation, and Backoff math.⚠️ The "Zeros" Problem (Sparsity)If a valid word sequence (e.g., "ruby slippers") never occurred in the training data, MLE assigns it a probability of 0. Because the Chain Rule multiplies probabilities together, one zero destroys the entire sentence calculation, resulting in and an undefined Perplexity (you can't divide by zero).Memory Hook 🧠How do we fix zeros? Call the FBI (but rearranged): Backoff: Use a shorter history if you lack data. Interpolation: Mix multiple histories together. Smoothing: Steal probability from seen events and give it to unseen events. Formula Explainer: Laplace (Add-1) SmoothingFormula: English Translation: Pretend you saw every single possible word combination exactly 1 extra time. You add to the numerator count. To keep the math valid (so probabilities still sum to 1), you must add to the denominator, where is the total number of unique words in your vocabulary. How to Solve: Given: The word "eat" appears 100 times. The bigram "eat chinese" appears 0 times. The vocabulary size is 2,000. Task: Find . Normal MLE would be: . Apply Laplace numerator: . Apply Laplace denominator: . Result: . (We successfully rescued the probability from being exactly zero!) 💡 Crucial Trap: V vs NA massive mistake students make is adding the total word count () to the denominator. No! You add the Vocabulary Size (), which is the number of unique word types. If a text has 1,000,000 words but only 5,000 unique words, .Formula Explainer: Reconstituted Counts ()Formula: English Translation: This tells you what the raw count would have to be in an unsmoothed model to get the same probability you just calculated via Laplace smoothing. It visualizes how much probability mass was "stolen" from frequent events. How to Solve: Following the previous example (, , ). . (The count changed from a 0 to a 0.047).Formula Explainer: Linear Interpolation & Stupid BackoffLaplace is a "blunt instrument" that steals too much mass. Modern systems use Interpolation or Backoff. 1. Linear Interpolation Formula: English: We mix Unigram, Bigram, and Trigram probabilities together. The (lambda) values are weights that must sum to 1 (e.g., 0.1, 0.3, 0.6). We learn the best values using a Held-out corpus. 2. Stupid Backoff: English: If you don't have enough data for a Trigram, drop down (back off) to the Bigram. If you don't have the Bigram, drop to the Unigram. Apply a fixed penalty weight (usually 0.4) every time you drop down. It is "stupid" because it doesn't create a perfect mathematical probability distribution, but it works incredibly well at web-scale.Comparison Table: Sparsity Solutions Use Case: Interpolation is highly preferred for combining different models (like mixing a general language model with an industry-specific medical language model).
item.guid: Natural Language Processing/Unit 3/Unit 3 Content/[Unit 3] - Smoothing, Interpolation & Backoff.md

item.title: Unit 3 - Bigram Example Solvings
item.description: #nlpGoal: Apply mathematical formulas for MLE, Laplace Smoothing, Reconstituted Counts, and Perplexity using a specific, fixed corpus.Our Training CorpusTo perform our calculations, we will use the following 4-sentence corpus. (Note: I have replaced your starting </s> with the standard start-of-sentence token <s> to make the bigram math perfectly accurate!) <s> she is with me </s> <s> i am with her </s> <s> she is with him </s> <s> but i am not with him </s> Essential Unigram Counts & Vocabulary Size () Before solving, we must count how many times each individual word appears. <s>: 4 she: 2 is: 2 with: 4 me: 1 i: 2 am: 2 her: 1 him: 2 but: 1 not: 1 </s>: 4 Vocabulary Size (): There are 11 unique words we can predict (excluding the <s> token, which is only a history context, not a predicted word). So, . Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) Formula: Let's calculate the raw MLE probabilities for a few specific bigrams found in our corpus. Example A: = 2 = 2 Result: 1.0 (100% of the time "she" appears, it is followed by "is") Example B: = 2 = 4 Result: 0.50 Example C: = 1 = 4 Result: 0.25 Laplace (Add-1) Smoothing Formula: (Remember: for our corpus) Let's smooth a bigram we did see, and a bigram we didn't see. Example A (Seen Bigram): Original MLE was . Let's smooth it. Numerator: Denominator: Result: 0.133 (Notice how the probability shrank to make room for unseen words!) Example B (Unseen Zero-Count Bigram): The phrase "she not" never appears in the corpus. Original MLE = 0. Numerator: Denominator: Result: 0.077 (We successfully rescued it from being zero!) Reconstituted Counts () Formula: This tells us what the raw count would have to be to get the Laplace probability naturally. Example A (Seen Bigram): We take our Laplace probability for "with me" (0.133) and multiply it by the raw count of "with" (4). 0.533 Insight: The original count was 1. Smoothing "stole" almost half of its count, reducing it to 0.533! Example B (Unseen Bigram): We take our Laplace probability for "she not" (0.077) and multiply it by the raw count of "she" (2). 0.154 Insight: The original count was 0. Smoothing "donated" 0.154 to it. Perplexity Calculation Formula: Let's test our unsmoothed MLE model on Sentence 3 from our corpus: Test Sentence (): <s> she is with him </s> Step 1: Calculate the Joint Probability of the sentence Multiply the bigram probabilities for every step: 0.25 Step 2: Find (Number of predicted tokens) The predicted tokens are: she, is, with, him, </s>. (We do not count <s> because it was just our starting context). * 5 Step 3: Calculate Perplexity 1.32 Final Insight: A perplexity of 1.32 is incredibly low (which is fantastic!), but that's only because we evaluated the model on the exact same sentence it was trained on. In the real world, testing on your training set is called "Data Contamination"!
item.guid: Natural Language Processing/Unit 3/Unit 3 Content/Unit 3 - Bigram Example Solvings.md

item.title: NLP Unit 3 Overview
item.description:
#nlpThis unit introduces the foundational mechanics of predicting upcoming words. You will transition from conceptual language properties into the hardcore probabilistic math used to train and evaluate Language Models (LMs).Unit NavigationClick the links below to view the specific reviewers:
[Unit 3] - N-Grams & Probability Estimation Topics: The Chain Rule, Markov Assumptions, Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE), Bigram Calculations
[Unit 3] - Evaluation & Perplexity Topics: Extrinsic vs. Intrinsic Evaluation, Train/Dev/Test sets, Perplexity Math
[Unit 3] - Smoothing, Interpolation & Backoff Topics: The Zeros Problem, Laplace (Add-1) Smoothing Math, Reconstituted Counts, Interpolation
Unit 3 - Bigram Example Solvings Example for solving bigrams and related formulas Key TakeawayA Language Model (LM) is ultimately just a probability distribution over word sequences. Because we cannot mathematically compute the true probability of an entire sentence due to its uniqueness, we use N-grams to approximate the probability by looking only at a short, fixed window of past words.
item.guid: Natural Language Processing/Unit 3/NLP Unit 3 Overview.md

item.title: [Unit 2] - Advanced Regex & Substitutions
item.description: #nlpGoal: Learn how to manipulate text by storing matches in memory, executing complex substitutions, and conditionally searching using lookaheads.Substitutions and Capture GroupsFormula (Python): re.sub(r"SearchPattern", r"ReplacementText", string) English Translation: Finds all matches of SearchPattern in the string and replaces them with ReplacementText. To rearrange text, we use Capture Groups ( ) to store matched data in memory slots (registers), and reference them in the replacement using \1, \2, etc. How to Solve (Date Swap Example): Task: Convert US Date MM/DD/YYYY to EU Date DD-MM-YYYY. Pattern: r"(\d{2})/(\d{2})/(\d{4})" Replacement: r"\2-\1-\3" Action: The month goes into Group 1, day into Group 2, year into Group 3. The replacement string calls Group 2 first, swapping the day and month! ⚠️ Non-Capturing GroupsSometimes you need to group items with parentheses for OR logic, but you don't want to store them in memory (to save registers/processing). Add ?: immediately inside the parenthesis: (?:pattern). Example: r"(?:some|a few) (cats|dogs) like \1" \1 refers to cats|dogs, NOT the first group, because ?: told the computer to ignore the first group.Lookahead AssertionsThese allow you to check for a pattern without advancing the search pointer (Zero-width). Simple Application: ELIZACreated by Joseph Weizenbaum (1966), ELIZA was an early NLP system that imitated a Rogerian psychotherapist. It relied entirely on regex capture groups and substitutions (pattern matching). How it works under the hood: User input: "I'm depressed much of the time." ELIZA's Regex: r".* I'M (depressed|sad) .*" ELIZA's Substitution: r"I AM SORRY TO HEAR YOU ARE \1" Output: "I AM SORRY TO HEAR YOU ARE depressed" Use Case: ELIZA proves that simple pattern-matching rules can create a shockingly convincing illusion of human understanding, forming the ancestor logic for modern rule-based chatbots.
item.guid: Natural Language Processing/Unit 2/Unit 2 Content/[Unit 2] - Advanced Regex & Substitutions.md

item.title: [Unit 2] - Corpora & Textual Variations
item.description: #nlpGoal: Understand the metadata, variability, and ethical documentation required when compiling massive text datasets.What is a Corpus?Words don't appear out of nowhere. A text is always produced by a specific writer, at a specific time, in a specific variety of a language, for a specific function. Memory Hook 🧠Remember the dimensions along which corpora vary with GLAD-V: Genre (Newswire, fiction, Wikipedia) Language (There are 7,097 languages in the world!) Author Demographics (Age, gender, socio-economic status) Domain/Function (Why was it written?) Variety (e.g., African American English "iont" instead of "I don't") Code SwitchingWhen speakers use multiple languages interchangeably in the same utterance. Where it happens: Very common globally, especially in spoken language, texting, and social media. Example (Spanish/English): "Por primera vez veo a @user actually being hateful!" Example (Hindi/English): "dost tha or ra- hega ... dont wory ..." 💡 Corpus DatasheetsA concept by Gebru et al. (2020) and Bender & Friedman (2018) arguing that every dataset should be documented to reveal biases. Key questions a datasheet must answer: Motivation: Why was it collected and by whom? Situation: In what context was the text written? Collection Process: Was there consent? How was it sampled? Use Case: Before training an LLM on scraped Reddit data, you must fill out a Corpus Datasheet to document potential demographic biases and consent issues.
item.guid: Natural Language Processing/Unit 2/Unit 2 Content/[Unit 2] - Corpora & Textual Variations.md

item.title: [Unit 2] - Regex Basics & Error Tradeoffs
item.description: #nlpGoal: Master the basic syntax of Regular Expressions and understand how to balance the two fundamental types of NLP errors.💡 Python Syntax: The Raw StringAlways use Python raw strings for regex: r"pattern". Why? Regex uses a lot of backslashes (\). In normal Python strings, \n means "newline". In raw strings, r"\n" strictly means the literal characters \ and n. It saves you from having to type double backslashes like "\\d+".Regex Syntax Cheatsheet⚠️ The Dual Personality of the Carat (^) If ^ is OUTSIDE brackets: It means Start of string/line. If ^ is the FIRST character INSIDE brackets [^...]: It means Negation (NOT). Example: r"[^A-Z]" means "Match any character that is NOT an uppercase letter." Gotcha: Inside brackets, special characters like . and * lose their special meaning. Regex AliasesQuick shortcuts for common character classes. Lowercase = Match, Uppercase = Negate. The Iterative Regex Process & NLP ErrorsWriting a regex is a constant battle between matching too much and matching too little. Let's say we want to find the word "the" in a text. r"the" Misses "The". This is a False Negative. r"[tT]he" Matches "The" and "the", but also matches "other" and "Theology". These are False Positives. r"\b[tT]he\b" Uses word boundaries (\b) to fix the false positives. TipUse Case: Building a spam filter. If valid emails go to spam (False Positive), users get angry (Low Precision). If spam gets into the inbox (False Negative), the filter is useless (Low Recall).
item.guid: Natural Language Processing/Unit 2/Unit 2 Content/[Unit 2] - Regex Basics & Error Tradeoffs.md

item.title: NLP Unit 2 Overview
item.description:
#nlpThis unit bridges the gap between raw linguistic data (corpora) and the programmatic tools used to extract, clean, and manipulate that data (Regular Expressions). It also introduces the fundamental trade-off in all NLP systems: balancing False Positives and False Negatives.Click the links below to view the specific reviewers:
[Unit 2] - Corpora & Textual Variations Topics: Dimensions of Corpora, Code Switching, Corpus Datasheets
[Unit 2] - Regex Basics & Error Tradeoffs Topics: Regex Syntax, Wildcards, Anchors, Aliases, Precision vs. Recall
[Unit 2] - Advanced Regex & Substitutions Topics: Capture Groups, Lookaheads, re.sub(), The ELIZA System Key TakeawayNatural language is messy and variable (code switching, different genres). To process this efficiently, we use Regular Expressions to define complex string patterns, but we constantly face a tug-of-war: making rules broad enough to catch all valid instances (Recall) without making them so broad that they catch garbage (Precision).
item.guid: Natural Language Processing/Unit 2/NLP Unit 2 Overview.md

item.title: [Unit 1] - Introduction to NLP & Applications
item.description: #nlpGoal: Understand what NLP is, how an NLP pipeline works, and identify its main real-world applications.What is NLP?Natural Language Processing is an area of AI and computer science concerned with translating human (natural) language into data (numbers) that a computer can process, act upon, or reply to. Natural Language vs. Programming Language: Natural languages are used for human-to-human sharing and are inherently ambiguous, whereas programming languages translate into finite mathematical operations. The NLP PipelineAn NLP system is called a "pipeline" because natural language flows into one end, goes through multiple sequential processing stages, and processed output flows out the other. Typical Spoken Dialogue Pipeline: Speech Analysis Morphological/Lexical Analysis Parsing Contextual Reasoning Application Execution Speech SynthesisCore NLP Tasks Classifying whole sentences. Classifying each word in a sentence. Generating text content. Extracting an answer from a text. Generating a new sentence from an input text. Common Applications of NLPEarly implementations focused on simply getting context/information from text. Modern applications utilize Deep Learning and Large Language Models (LLMs) to understand and generate complex human-like responses. Memory Hook 🧠Remember the major NLP applications with the acronym G-CAMPS: Generative AI (ChatGPT, DALL-E) Chatbots (Mimic human conversation) Assistants (Siri, Alexa - infer meaning from speech) Machine Translation (Google Translate - breaks language barriers) Predictive Text (Autocomplete, autocorrect) Search Engines (Understanding intent behind queries) Spam Filters (Classifying unwanted emails) TipUse Case: Building a virtual customer service agent that can classify a user's problem (Spam vs Valid query), translate their language (MT), and generate a helpful response (Generative AI/Chatbot).
item.guid: Natural Language Processing/Unit 1/Unit 1 contents/[Unit 1] - Introduction to NLP & Applications.md

item.title: [Unit 1] - Textual Sources & NLP Packages
item.description: #nlpGoal: Know where to acquire linguistic data (corpora) for training models and identify the best Python libraries for processing that data.Textual Sources (Corpora)A text corpus (plural: corpora) is a large, structured body of text used for practical NLP work and model training. Memory Hook 🧠To remember the three classic academic corpora, think of B.R.G. (Big Reading Groups): Brown Corpus: First 1M-word electronic English corpus (1961). Categorized by genre (news, fiction, hobbies). Reuters Corpus: 10,788 news documents (1.3M words). Classified into 90 topics (grouped into train/test sets). Gutenberg Project: Archive of over 25,000 free electronic books. TipModern Sources: Kaggle, Hugging Face datasets, Web Scraping (extracting data from HTML using scripts). Use Case: Training a sentiment analysis model on movie reviews scraped directly from a website.NLP Packages in PythonDifferent tasks require different libraries. The two most prominent are NLTK and spaCy. 💡 Best PracticeIf you need to strictly test a new linguistic theory, use NLTK. If you just need the end-result quickly for a production web app, use spaCy.
item.guid: Natural Language Processing/Unit 1/Unit 1 contents/[Unit 1] - Textual Sources & NLP Packages.md

item.title: [Unit 1] - Tokenization Techniques
item.description: #nlpGoal: Understand how to segment text into tokens using modern data-driven (BPE) and traditional rule-based methods.⚠️ Why traditional "words" fail NLP Many languages don't use spaces (Chinese, Japanese). Word definitions are subjective (Chinese Hanzi/morpheme segmentation varies wildly). The number of content words grows infinitely (Unknown Word problem). Solution: NLP uses Subword Tokenization instead of whole words. Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE) Token Learner AlgorithmThe NLP standard for subword tokenization. It uses the training data to tell the system how to tokenize, effectively solving the "unknown word" problem. How it works (Training Phase): Add a special end-of-word symbol _ to space-separated words. Split all words into individual characters (this is the initial vocabulary). Iterative Merge: Find the most frequent pair of adjacent tokens in the corpus. Merge them to create a new, single token (e.g., e + r er). Add this new token to the vocabulary and replace all instances in the corpus. Repeat for merges. BPE Testing Phase (Crucial Concept)To tokenize a new (test) sentence, you apply the merge rules learned during training greedily, in the exact order they were learned. Note: The frequencies of the characters in the TEST set do NOT matter. Only the sequence of rules learned from the TRAINING set matters.TipUse Case: BPE is the exact tokenization method used by LLMs like GPT-4 to handle millions of unique words without crashing out on "Out of Vocabulary" errors.Rule-Based TokenizationSometimes, strict grammar rules are required for parsing or social science tasks. Challenges: Punctuation rules (e.g., m.p.h., Ph.D., $45.55), URLs, Clitics ('s), Multiword expressions (New York). Penn Treebank Standard: Standardized way to separate punctuation and clitics (e.g., "doesn't" does, n't). NLTK RegEx: Uses verbose regular expressions to define what constitutes a valid word token. Space-based: Simplest method (Unix tr). Only works for languages with spaces. Sentence SegmentationThe problem of determining where a sentence ends. ! and ? are generally unambiguous. . (period) is highly ambiguous (End of sentence? Abbreviation Dr.? Number 4.3?). Standard Algorithm: Tokenize the text first (using ML or rules). Classify each period: is it part of a word/abbreviation, or a sentence boundary? Segment the sentence based on the classification.
item.guid: Natural Language Processing/Unit 1/Unit 1 contents/[Unit 1] - Tokenization Techniques.md

item.title: [Unit 1] - Words, Tokens & Morphology
item.description: #nlpGoal: Master the distinction between types and instances, calculate vocabulary growth, and understand how words are built (morphology) across different human languages.Types vs. Instances & Content vs. Function Type: An element of the vocabulary . (Unique words). Instance (Token): An occurrence of that type in running text. (Total word count ). Example: "They lay on the grass and looked at the stars." The word "the" is 1 Type but 2 Instances. Heap's Law (Herdan's Law) 🧠Estimates the number of unique items (Types) in your vocabulary based on document length. English Translation: The size of your vocabulary () is equal to a constant multiplier (, usually 10-100) times the total number of words read so far () raised to a power (, usually 0.4 to 0.6). Conceptually: Vocabulary size goes up roughly with the square root of its length in words. It never strictly stops growing (because of new content words/names). How to Solve / Interpret: If you double the length of a book (), your unique word count () does not double; it grows much slower due to the exponent.Morphology (Word Parts)Morpheme: The minimal meaning-bearing unit in a language (e.g., cats = cat + s). Root: Central morpheme supplying main meaning. Affix: Adds additional meaning. Inflectional: Grammatical role (e.g., -ed past tense, -s plural). Derivational: Changes meaning or grammatical class (e.g., care + ful = careful). ⚠️ The Clitic TrapA clitic acts syntactically like a word but is reduced and attached to another word (e.g., 've in I've, 's in She's). Gotcha: Orthographically (in writing), it looks like one word. Grammatically, it is two words. Tokenizers must split them!Morphological Typology (Greenberg Scale)Languages vary by (1) Morphemes per word, and (2) How easily segmentable they are. Segmentation Ease: Agglutinative: Clean boundaries between morphemes (Turkish). Fusion: A single affix conflates multiple morphemes (e.g., Russian -om means singular + instrumental + first declension).
item.guid: Natural Language Processing/Unit 1/Unit 1 contents/[Unit 1] - Words, Tokens & Morphology.md

item.title: Byte Pair Encoding (BPE) Example Solvings
item.description: #nlpGoal: Manually apply the Byte Pair Encoding (BPE) algorithm to a specific corpus to understand how subword tokenization learns its vocabulary.Our Training Corpus & Initial SetupRaw Corpus: the cat sat on the mat (We will treat the_cat_sat_on_the_mat as individual words separated by spaces). Step 1: Add End-of-Word Tokens & Get Counts We append a special symbol (like _) to the end of every word, and count how many times each word appears in the corpus. the_ : 2 cat_ : 1 sat_ : 1 on_ : 1 mat_ : 1 Step 2: Split into Characters (Initial State) We break all words down into their individual characters. This forms our starting vocabulary. Initial Vocabulary: a, c, e, h, m, n, o, s, t, _ Corpus State: t h e _ : 2 c a t _ : 1 s a t _ : 1 o n _ : 1 m a t _ : 1 BPE Iteration 1: Finding the most frequent pairThe Algorithm: Find the most frequent adjacent pair of tokens in the entire corpus and merge them. Let's look at the counts of adjacent pairs in our current state: t h appears 2 times (in the_) h e appears 2 times e _ appears 2 times a t appears 3 times (in cat_, sat_, mat_) t _ appears 3 times (in cat_, sat_, mat_) Since a and t appear next to each other 3 times, we merge them into at! New Token Added to Vocab: at Updated Corpus State: t h e _ : 2 c at _ : 1 s at _ : 1 o n _ : 1 m at _ : 1 BPE Iteration 2: The next mergeNow we look at our updated corpus state and find the next most frequent adjacent pair. t h = 2 h e = 2 at _ = 3 (in c at _, s at _, m at _) We merge at and _ into at_! New Token Added to Vocab: at_ Updated Corpus State: t h e _ : 2 c at_ : 1 s at_ : 1 o n _ : 1 m at_ : 1 BPE Iteration 3 & 4: Cleaning up "the"Let's fast forward through the next two logical merges. Looking at our state, the most frequent pairs left all belong to the word the_, which has a count of 2. Iteration 3 Merge: t and h (Count = 2) Vocab Adds: th State: th e _ (count: 2) Iteration 4 Merge: th and e (Count = 2) Vocab Adds: the State: the _ (count: 2) (We could optionally do Iteration 5 to merge the and _ into the_).Final Takeaway: The Testing PhaseWhy did we do this? Notice that our system learned the token at_. If we give the model a completely unseen test word, like bat or hat, it won't crash! It will simply break it down into b + at_ or h + at_. The Rule: During testing, you do not recount frequencies. You simply apply the merge rules you learned (1. a+t, 2. at+_, 3. t+h...) in the exact same greedy order to the new text.
item.guid: Natural Language Processing/Unit 1/Unit 1 contents/Byte Pair Encoding (BPE) Example Solvings.md

item.title: NLP Unit 1 Overview
item.description:
#nlpThis unit covers the foundational concepts of Natural Language Processing (NLP), from high-level applications and textual data sources down to the grammatical and algorithmic breakdown of words into machine-readable tokens.Click the links below to view the specific reviewers:
[Unit 1] - Introduction to NLP & Applications Topics: NLP Definition, NLP Pipeline, Core Tasks, Common Applications
[Unit 1] - Textual Sources & NLP Packages Topics: Corpora (Gutenberg, Brown, Reuters), Web Scraping, NLTK vs. spaCy
[Unit 1] - Words, Tokens & Morphology Topics: Types vs. Instances, Heap's Law, Morphemes, Morphological Typology
[Unit 1] - Tokenization Techniques Topics: Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE), Rule-based Tokenization, Sentence Segmentation
Byte Pair Encoding (BPE) Example Solvings Example problem for solving BPE Key TakeawayThe fundamental challenge of NLP is translating fluid, ambiguous human language into structured, deterministic mathematical data. Because human language is infinitely generative and messy (e.g., clitics, unknown words, lack of spaces in some languages), traditional word-level processing fails. The modern solution is subword tokenization driven by data (like BPE) rather than rigid grammatical rules.
item.guid: Natural Language Processing/Unit 1/NLP Unit 1 Overview.md

item.title: Unit 1 - Analytics Types and Applications
item.description: #CCS230 #Unit1 #Analytics_Applications #data_mining Analytics Types and ApplicationsGoal: Master the four levels of analytics and understand how they translate into tangible business value across various industries.📖 The Four Types of AnalyticsData analysis escalates in complexity and value across four distinct types: Descriptive: Characterizes general properties (what is happening). Diagnostic: Identifies anomalies/discrepancies (why it happened). Predictive: Performs induction to make forecasts (what will happen). Prescriptive: Discovers actionable patterns for Business Intelligence (how can we make it happen). Memory Hook 🧠Acronym: DDPP D Descriptive (What?) D Diagnostic (Why?) P Predictive (Future?) P Prescriptive (Action?) 📖 Practical ApplicationsData mining is applied differently depending on the industry goal: Market Basket Analysis: Finding items bought together. Use Case: Placing diapers next to baby food to increase cross-sales. Fraud Detection: Using outlier analysis for credit cards. Use Case: Flagging an unusually large transaction in a foreign country. CRM (Customer Relationship Management): Cluster analysis for segmentation. Use Case: Identifying a "big spender" demographic to launch targeted marketing. Sentiment Analysis: Mining social media text. Use Case: Gauging public opinion on a new product launch via Twitter comments. Quick Check ❓ Which type of analytics uses "data auditing tools" to perform root-cause investigations? If a company optimizes its supply chain to reduce costs based on data, which of the four analytics types are they executing? How is churn prediction applied in the telecommunications industry?
item.guid: Data Mining/Midterms Reviewer/Reviewer Content/Unit 1/Unit 1 Content/Unit 1 - Analytics Types and Applications.md

item.title: Unit 1 - KDD and Core Tasks
item.description: #CCS230 #Unit1 #KDD_TasksKDD and Core TasksGoal: Understand the systematic lifecycle of extracting knowledge from data and distinguish between the two primary categories of data mining tasks.📖 Data Mining & KDDData mining is the computational process of automatically discovering valid, novel, useful, and understandable patterns within massive data repositories. It is the algorithmic engine of Knowledge Discovery in Databases (KDD). Don't Confuse TheseDo not use "Data Mining" and "KDD" entirely interchangeably. KDD is the holistic, iterative process, whereas data mining is just the specific algorithmic phase within that larger process.📖 The KDD LifecycleTurning data tombs into knowledge requires rigorous preparation before actual mining occurs: Selection & Integration: Identifying sources and merging disparate data (web logs, databases). Cleaning & Preprocessing: Removing noise, handling duplicates, and fixing inconsistencies. Transformation: Converting data into suitable formats via normalization or aggregation. Pattern Evaluation & Knowledge Presentation: Assessing validity and visualizing insights via dashboards. Memory HookAcronym: SCTP (Steps to extract knowledge) S Selection (and Integration) C Cleaning (and Preprocessing) T Transformation P Pattern Evaluation (and Knowledge Presentation) Comparison: Predictive vs. Descriptive ModelingData mining functionalities are generally divided into two main categories: predictive and descriptive. Quick Check What is the most labor-intensive stage of the KDD lifecycle? If I want to group customers based on purchasing behavior without having predefined categories, am I using predictive or descriptive modeling? What is the difference between Classification and Regression?
item.guid: Data Mining/Midterms Reviewer/Reviewer Content/Unit 1/Unit 1 Content/Unit 1 - KDD and Core Tasks.md

item.title: Template
item.description:
You are an expert academic note-maker specializing in Obsidian markdown reviewer pages optimized for deep memorization and active recall. Your output uses the Obsidian Admonition plugin syntax (ad-* blocks).Given source material (lecture notes, textbooks, slides, etc.) for a specific topic or unit, generate: One child reviewer file per sub-topic provided. One parent overview file that links all child files together. Use blocks with strict semantic purpose — never use a block type just for visual variety. Outermost block (ad-info): Always use collapse: open. This is the only block that should have collapse. Second level (e.g. ad-note, ad-summary): No collapse. These are always visible within the section. Third level (e.g. ad-warning, ad-tip, ad-example nested inside a note): Use 5-backtick fences ad-type. Never nest more than 3 levels deep. If you're going deeper, break it into a separate section. Fence depth reference:Section TitleKey ConceptMemory Hook 🧠...Apply these wherever appropriate. Use judgment — not every rule applies to every concept.When a list must be memorized, create a mnemonic.Place it in an ad-tip block titled "Memory Hook 🧠" immediately after the list it refers to.Format:InfoAcronym: WORD W → First item O → Second item R → Third item D → Fourth item Structure every formula block as follows inside an ad-note:ImportantFormula: English Translation: Plain-language explanation of what each component does and why the formula works this way. Variables: → what it represents → what it represents Worked Example: Short concrete walkthrough — plug in numbers and solve step by step.Always bold the most critical word or phrase in each callout block.Always use a markdown table. Include only columns that differentiate the concepts — omit shared properties.Example structure:SummaryPlace the table inside an ad-summary block titled "Comparison: A vs. B".When a concept has a specific real-world application, end its block with: Use Case: [one-sentence real-world application] For every major concept, add at least one ad-question block at the end of its section.Format:Quick Check [Question testing the core mechanism] [Question testing a common misconception] [Optional: applied/scenario question] The first line of every file must be tags. The first tag must be the subject/course.#subject_name #unit_name #subtopic_keywordFilename: [Unit X] - [Sub-topic Name].md
#course #unitX #subtopic_keyword[Sub-topic Name]Goal: [One sentence — what does mastering this sub-topic enable you to do or understand?] 📖 [First Key Concept][Definition or explanation — 2–4 sentences max. Bold the key term on first use.] Common Mistake ⚠️[The most common error students make with this concept.]Memory Hook[Acronym or mnemonic if applicable.][Second Key Concept or Formula][Breakdown of concept or formula using Rule 2 structure.]Worked Example[Step-by-step example. Use numbered steps. Be explicit.]Quick Check [Recall question] [Application question] Filename: [Unit X] - Overview.md
#course #unitX #overview[2–3 sentence framing paragraph: What is this unit about? Why does it matter? What is the overarching theme?]Unit NavigationCore Takeaway[The single most important insight from this entire unit. Answer: "So what? Why does all of this matter?"]Unit-Level Review [Broad synthesis question spanning multiple sub-topics] [Question requiring you to connect two concepts from different child files]
Quick Links: Child File 1 · Child File 2 · Child File 3 Bold key terms on first use only. Do not over-bold. Italics for nuance, caveats, and "in other words" clarifications. Use $...$ for inline math and $$...$$ for block equations. Never write formulas as plain text. Keep bullet points to 1–2 lines. If a point needs more, use a sub-block instead. Prefer tables over long prose for comparisons. Never write long paragraphs — break them into labeled sub-blocks. Every formula must have an English Translation (Rule 2). No exceptions. Every major concept must have at least one ad-question check. Aim for scannable, not exhaustive — the notes should feel like flashcards, not a textbook. The key improvements made: Nesting logic is now explicit — fence depth (4/5/6 backticks) is documented with a visual reference, removing all ambiguity Block semantics are sharper — ad-danger, ad-failure, ad-bug are now distinguished from ad-warning with clear use cases Formula rule is more structured — the "Variables" and "Worked Example" subsections are now required fields, not optional Active recall is formalized as Rule 6 — every concept now requires a ad-question block, turning the notes into a retrieval-practice tool Parent file uses a table for navigation instead of a list, making it cleaner to scan Comparison tables have a placement rule — always inside ad-summary, always titled "Comparison: A vs. B" Style rules are more specific and actionable (e.g. "bold key terms on first use only")
item.guid: Template/Template.md

item.title: SIPP Overview
item.description:
#sippThis master overview bridges the foundational concepts of IT ethics (Unit 1) with the broader historical, economic, and social impacts of computing technology (Unit 2). Together, these units form the basis for understanding how technology shapes human society and the responsibilities of the professionals building it.Course Navigation (Units 1 & 2)Click the links below to access the unit-level overviews and their specific sub-topic reviewers: Focus: Ethical Frameworks, Dilemmas, and Professional Responsibility
👉 SIPP Unit 1 Overview
[Unit 1] - Motivating Scenarios
[Unit 1] - Framework for Ethical Analysis
[Unit 1] - Aims of the Course Focus: The Network Society, Automation, Inequality, and Cyber-Warfare SIPP Unit 2 Overview Part 1: History & Communication
[Unit 2] - A Very Short History of IT
[Unit 2] - Transition in Communication Part 2: Labor, Equality & Infrastructure
[Unit 2] - Work and Employment Transformed
[Unit 2] - Digital Divide and Bias
[Unit 2] - Interactive Media and Governance Part 3: Dual-Use Technology
[Unit 2] - ICT for Peace and Warfare Master Key TakeawayComputing is not isolated from society; it shapes and is shaped by it. Unit 1 gives you the ethical framework (how to make rational, principled decisions), while Unit 2 provides the social context (the historical, economic, and political realities your technology will alter). To be a competent IT professional, you must apply the ethical tools from Unit 1 to navigate the complex social dilemmas presented in Unit 2.

item.title: Unit 2 - Introduction to Data, Measurements, and Data preprocessing
item.description: #data_miningThe Quality BottleneckGarbage In, Garbage OutYou cannot cook a Michelin-star meal with rotten ingredients. Up to 80% of data science/mining is just cleaning. Why Preprocessing? Accuracy: Handling Noise Completeness: Handling Missing Values Consistency: Handling Discrepancies Data ObjectsDefinition: Represents a specific entity. Contextual Examples Sales DB: Customers, store items, sales. Medical DB: Patients. University DB: Students, professors, courses. Synonyms for Data ObjectsYou may see these terms used interchangeably: Examples Instances Data Points Objects Data AttributesDefinition: A data field representing a characteristic or feature of a data object. One Concept, Many Names Dimension: Data Warehousing Attribute: Data Mining & Databases Feature: Machine Learning Variable: Statistics The Taxonomy of AttributesThere are four specific types of attributes you must know. Nominal Attributes "Names" Values represent categories, codes, or states. Also called: Categorical. Math: Mode only. Example: Blood Type (A, B, AB, O), Color (Red, Blue). Binary Attributes "0 or 1" Nominal attributes with only two states. Boolean: If states are True/False. Types of Binary: Symmetric: Both states carry equal weight (e.g., Gender). Asymmetric: Outcomes are not equally important. The rarer/important outcome is usually coded as 1 (e.g., HIV test result). Ordinal Attributes "Ranked" Values have a meaningful order. Constraint: The magnitude between values is unknown. Example: Drink Sizes (Small, Medium, Large), Disease Stage (I, II, III). Numeric Attributes "Quantitative" Measurable quantities (Integer or Real values). Scales: Interval-scaled: Equal-size units, NO true zero-point (e.g., Temperature in Celsius). Ratio-scaled: Inherent zero-point, allows multiples/ratios (e.g., Salary, Height). Continuity: Discrete: Finite or countably infinite set (Integers). Continuous: Real numbers (Floating-point).
item.guid: Data Mining/Unit 2 - Introduction to Data, Measurements, and Data preprocessing.md

item.title: Unit 2 - Statistics of Data
item.description: #data_miningMeasures of Central TendencyGoal: Measure the location of the middle or center of a data distribution. "Where do most values fall?" The Three Measures of Central TendencyMean (The Balance Point)Formula: The weighted center. Pros: Most common measure. Cons: Highly sensitive to outliers (e.g., a billionaire enters a room). Median (The Border Divider)Definition: The middle value when data is sorted ( percentile). Position: Pros: Robust statistic (ignored outliers). Use Case: Skewed data (Income, House Prices). Approximated Medianused in massive datasets where sorting the entire set is computationally expensive Mode (The Popular Vote)Definition: The value that appears most frequently. Types: Unimodal, Bimodal, Multimodal. Pros: The only measure for Categorical/Nominal data. Use Case: Imputing missing categorical values. Data Distribution (Skewness)Skewness measures the asymmetry of the probability distribution. Pearson's Coefficient of Skewness: Logic: If Skewness > 1 or < -1, transformations (Log/Square Root) are needed for ML algorithms. Measures of DispersionGoal: Quantify the "Chaos". How are points scattered around the center? Basic MeasuresRange (The Extremes)Formula: The simplest measure of spread. Pros: Easy to calculate. Cons: Extremely sensitive to outliers as it only uses the two most extreme values. Percentiles ()Definition: Divides sorted data into 100 equal-sized groups. The -th percentile is the value below which of the data falls. Key Values: : The Median. : The threshold for the "Top 1%". Quartiles ()Definition: Three specific points that divide the ordered data set into four equal parts (quarters). ( Quartile / Percentile): Splits off the lowest 25% of data. ( Quartile / Percentile): The Median. ( Quartile / Percentile): Splits off the highest 25% (lowest 75%). The Boxplot (Five-Number Summary)Used to visualize distribution and outliers. The 5 Points: Minimum Median () Maximum Interquartile Range (IQR): Formula: Represents the middle 50% of data. The Outlier Test (Fences): Lower Fence: Upper Fence: Any data outside these fences is an outlier. Variance & Standard DeviationVariance (): Average squared distance from the mean. (Note the "Bessel's Correction" for sample data) Standard Deviation (): Interpretation: Low SD = clustered data; High SD = wide spread.
item.guid: Data Mining/Unit 2 - Statistics of Data.md
